title: Data Structures & Algorithms — Staff+ FAANG Interview Prep
description: Data Structures & Algorithms at Staff+ FAANG depth. Covers complexity analysis, advanced graph algorithms, dynamic programming optimization, segment trees, tries, consistent hashing, bloom filters, concurrent data structures, memory hierarchy-aware algorithms, randomized/streaming algorithms, online algorithms, NP-completeness, approximation algorithms, DSA in system design context, and interview strategy.

sections.title: 1. Foundations: Time & Space Complexity Mastery
sections.description:
FOUNDATIONS · COMPLEXITY · AMORTIZED · PRODUCTION

Staff+ expectation: Big O is table stakes. At Staff+, you reason about complexity across the full system — not just the algorithm in isolation, but with memory hierarchy, concurrency, network overhead, and real-world input distributions. You choose algorithms knowing the production constraints: cache misses matter more than CPU cycles at scale.

Amortized Analysis: The cost of n operations divided by n. Critical for understanding data structures where occasional expensive operations are balanced by many cheap ones (e.g., dynamic array resizing, splay trees). Three methods: (1) Aggregate — bound total cost of n operations. (2) Accounting — assign different "amortized costs" to operations. (3) Potential — use a potential function. Staff+ insight: amortized analysis is not just theoretical — it explains why Python list.append() is O(1) amortized despite occasional O(n) resizes, and why this matters for real-time systems where tail latency is critical.

Real-World Complexity: Big O ignores constants. In production, constants dominate at every scale that fits in memory. O(n log n) with poor cache locality beats O(n log n) with perfect cache locality on modern hardware. At FAANG, measurement always beats intuition: profile first, optimize second. The Theorems of Big O break down when: (1) n is small (constants dominate), (2) input has adversarial structure, (3) data doesn't fit in L1/L2/L3 cache.

Big O
Worst-case asymptotic bound
Theta
Tight asymptotic bound
Omega
Best-case asymptotic bound
Amortized
Average over sequence of ops

Staff+ Mindset: When writing a distributed sorting pipeline, don't ask "what's the complexity?" Ask "what's the total bytes moved across the network?" Complexity analysis changes when data doesn't fit in memory or in a single machine. Use external sorting (mergesort variant) when data exceeds RAM. Use sampling before sorting for massive datasets.

sections.title: 2. Arrays & Strings at Scale
sections.description:
ARRAYS · STRINGS · TWO-POINTERS · SLIDING WINDOW

Staff+ expectation: Array and string problems are the most common interview category. At Staff+, you need to solve them with optimal time AND space, handle streaming/large-data variants, and understand when to use specialized hardware (SIMD, GPUs).

Two-Pointer Technique: Classic for sorted arrays (pair sum, three-sum, container with most water). Staff+ insight: think about why two-pointer works — it exploits monotonicity. If the pointer movement guarantees we never miss the optimal solution, the algorithm is correct. This generalizes to more complex problems: the "next greater element" stack-based algorithm is conceptually a two-pointer with an auxiliary stack.

Sliding Window: Fixed-size and variable-size windows. Staff+ expectation: handle duplicates in O(1) space, handle character encodings, handle out-of-order data. Substring-with-concatenation problems require two-level hashing. At FAANG, sliding window is the foundation for network packet inspection, rate limiting, and streaming analytics.

String Algorithms at Scale: (1) KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) — O(n+m) pattern matching, no backtracking. (2) Rabin-Karp — rolling hash with modulus to avoid overflow. Double-hash to reduce collision probability to near-zero. (3) Z-algorithm — compute longest prefix match at each position in O(n). Staff+ insight: for production text search, you don't implement KMP. You use suffix arrays (O(n log n) build, O(|P| + log n) search) or suffix trees (O(n) build, O(|P|) search).

KMPRabin-KarpZ-algorithmBoyer-MooreSuffix array
AlgorithmTimeSpaceUse caseFAANG relevance
O(n+m)O(m)Pattern matchingGoogle Search, Docs
O(n+m) avgO(1)Plagiarism detectionCode Search
O(n)O(n)String preprocessingGenome indexing
O(n/m) bestO(1)Text editorsVS Code, emacs
O(n log n)O(n)Substring search (fast)Google Search

Parallel Array Processing: Arrays larger than memory need out-of-core techniques: (1) Sort then stream. (2) Hash-based partitioning. (3) Bloom filter pre-filtering. At FAANG, MapReduce / Spark are the standard tools for massive array transformations. A Staff+ engineer knows when to use SIMD (x86 AVX-512 processes 16 int32 ops per instruction) for in-memory array operations.

sections.title: 3. Linked Lists & Trees in Production Systems
sections.description:
LINKED LISTS · TREES · BST · HEAP · PRODUCTION

Staff+ expectation: Linked lists and trees are the foundation of real-world data structures: LSM trees (LevelDB/RocksDB), B-trees (PostgreSQL/MySQL), and lock-free linked lists (concurrent queues). At Staff+, you understand why functional correctness and concurrency behavior matter more than pointer manipulation tricks.

Lock-Free Linked Lists (Michael-Scott Queue, 1996): Standard concurrent queue. Uses compare-and-swap (CAS) on the tail pointer. Insert: create new node, CAS tail.next from null to new node, CAS tail from old to new. At FAANG, every major language runtime uses lock-free linked lists for work-stealing queues, channel buffers, and async task queues.

Skip Lists: Probabilistic alternative to balanced trees. O(log n) with high probability. Redis uses skip lists for sorted sets. LevelDB uses skip lists for in-memory memtables. Staff+ insight: skip lists are easier to implement lock-free than red-black trees. The tradeoff is memory overhead (duplicate pointers) vs simplicity.

Trees in Production: B-trees power all major databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite). B+ trees power InnoDB and WiredTiger. LSM trees power LevelDB, RocksDB (Meta), Bigtable (Google), Cassandra, HBase. Staff+ understanding: B-trees optimize for disk (high fanout, fewer I/Os), LSM trees optimize for write throughput (sequential writes to immutable sorted files). The choice is foundational in database architecture — B-tree for read-heavy, LSM for write-heavy workloads.

B-treeB+ treeLSM treeSkip listTreap
StructureReadWriteSpaceProduction use
O(log_B n)O(log_B n)O(n)PostgreSQL, MySQL
O(log_B n)O(log_B n)O(n)InnoDB, SQLite
O(log n)O(log n) amortizedO(n) + compactionRocksDB, Bigtable, Cassandra
O(log n) avgO(log n) avgO(n log n)Redis SortedSet
O(log n) avgO(log n) avgO(n)Randomized BST

Binary Tree Interview Patterns: (1) Tree traversals — iterative (stack-based) for O(1) space (Morris traversal). (2) Lowest Common Ancestor — binary lifting for O(log n) per query after O(n log n) preprocessing. (3) Serialization — use level-order with markers, handles null children explicitly. (4) Trees as graphs — "all nodes distance K" is a BFS/graph problem, not just a tree problem.

sections.title: 4. Advanced Graph Algorithms
sections.description:
GRAPHS · SHORTEST PATH · TOPOLOGICAL · UNION-FIND

Staff+ expectation: Graph algorithms are the most common "hard" interview topic. At Staff+, you need to handle graphs that don't fit in memory, graphs with edge updates, and probabilistic graphs. You also need to recognize when a problem is fundamentally a graph problem (dependency resolution, circuit design, social network analysis) and choose the right algorithm.

Shortest Path in Practice: Dijkstra (O(E + V log V) with Fibonacci heap) is the workhorse. But at FAANG scale: (1) Road networks use A* with landmarks + contraction hierarchies (Google Maps processes 100M+ route queries/day). (2) Network routing uses OSPF (Dijkstra) + BGP (path vector). (3) Social graphs use bidirectional BFS (Facebook friend distance: O(b^(d/2)) vs O(b^d)). Bellman-Ford is used when edges have negative weights but no negative cycles — at FAANG, negative edge weights appear in arbitrage detection and currency conversion problems.

Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union): O(alpha(n)) per operation with path compression + union by rank. Staff+ insight: union-find is the simplest correct algorithm for dynamic connectivity. But it does NOT support edge deletions — for that you need Euler tour trees or dynamic connectivity (Holm, de Lichtenberg, Thorup). Union-find with rollbacks supports undo operations via a stack of changes — critical for offline dynamic connectivity (divide and conquer on time intervals).

Topological Sort: Kahn's algorithm (BFS-based) vs DFS-based. Kahn's is preferred at FAANG because it detects cycles and processes nodes in dependency order naturally. Staff+ insight: topological sort generalizes to scheduling problems: task scheduling with resource constraints, build system dependency resolution (Bazel, Buck, Pants), and package manager dependency ordering (pip, npm, cargo).

DijkstraBellman-FordFloyd-WarshallPrim's MSTKruskal's MST
AlgorithmTimeSpaceUse caseFAANG
O(E + V log V)O(V)Shortest path, non-negativeMaps (A* variant)
O(VE)O(V)Shortest path, negative edgesArbitrage detection
O(V^3)O(V^2)All-pairs shortestSmall graphs only
O(E log V)O(V)Minimum spanning treeNetwork design
O(E log E)O(E)Minimum spanning treeCluster (DBSCAN)

Staff+ Graph Interview Strategy: (1) Always clarify: directed vs undirected, weighted vs unweighted, dense vs sparse, static vs dynamic. (2) When stuck: try DFS (is it a tree traversal?), then BFS (shortest path in unweighted?), then Dijkstra (shortest path in weighted?), then DP on DAG (topological sort + DP). (3) For "minimum" or "maximum" — think MST, min-cut, max-flow. (4) For "detect" — think DFS with colors, union-find, or topological sort.

sections.title: 5. Advanced Dynamic Programming
sections.description:
DYNAMIC PROGRAMMING · OPTIMIZATION · STAFF+

Staff+ expectation: DP separates good engineers from great ones. At Staff+, you not only solve DP problems — you recognize when DP is applicable (optimal substructure + overlapping subproblems), choose the right DP formulation (top-down vs bottom-up, state compression), and optimize for real-world constraints (time, space, parallelism).

Four-Step DP Framework: (1) Define state — what does dp[i][j] represent? (2) Find recurrence — how does dp[i][j] relate to previous states? (3) Identify base cases — what are the simplest subproblems? (4) Order of computation — does it depend on previous, diagonal, or all states? Staff+ insight: getting the state definition right is 80% of solving any DP problem. A bad state definition leads to exponential time; a good one leads to polynomial time.

DP Optimization Techniques (Crucial for Staff+): (1) State compression — reduce 2D DP to 1D when recurrence only depends on previous row (knapsack, LCS, edit distance). (2) Divide and Conquer DP Optimization — for DP of form dp[i][j] = min_{k

LinearIntervalTreeBitmaskDigit DPDP on DAG
DP TypeClassic ProblemTimeOptimization
House robber, FibonacciO(n)O(1) space: store last 2
Matrix chain, Burst balloonsO(n^3)Knuth: O(n^2)
Diameter, Tree coloringO(n)DFS + memo
TSP, AssignmentO(n * 2^n)Meet-in-middle: O(n * 2^(n/2))
Count numbers with propertyO(digits * state)Memoization
Longest path in DAGO(V+E)Topological order

Top-Down vs Bottom-Up: Top-down (recursion + memo) is easier to reason about and computes only needed states. Bottom-up (tabulation) avoids recursion overhead and is easier to optimize with state compression. At FAANG, prefer bottom-up for production code (no stack overflow risk, better cache locality). Use top-down during interviews for clarity, then optimize to bottom-up.

Staff+ Edge Cases: DP with negative numbers, DP modulo large prime (handle negative modulo), DP for streaming data (online DP), DP with concurrency (parallelizing independent subproblems). At FAANG scale, you may need to distribute DP computation — MapReduce or Spark for DP over massive state spaces.

sections.title: 6. Segment Trees, Fenwick Trees & Range Queries
sections.description:
RANGE QUERIES · SEGMENT TREE · FENWICK · RMQ

Staff+ expectation: Range query data structures solve the most common class of "hard" problems: range sum, range min/max, range update, and range update + range query. At Staff+, you choose between segment tree (versatile), Fenwick tree (simpler, faster, limited to prefix operations), and sparse table (immutable, O(1) query).

Segment Tree: Full binary tree over an array. Each node represents a segment. O(log n) per query/update. Staff+ variants: (1) Lazy propagation — range updates in O(log n) by deferring updates to children. (2) Persistent segment tree — support querying historical versions (used in git-like data structures, range k-th order statistic). (3) Segment tree on 2D — O(log^2 n) per query, used for rectangle queries. (4) Dynamic segment tree — sparse, O(log MAX) per operation, no coordinate compression needed.

Fenwick Tree (Binary Indexed Tree): Simpler than segment tree, O(log n) per query/update. Only supports prefix operations (sum, min, max) and point updates. Can be extended to range updates + range queries with two BITs. Staff+ insight: Fenwick trees use less memory (n vs 4n for segment tree), have better constants, and are always preferred when they suffice. Google's internal data structures library uses BITs for scrolling metrics, streaming percentiles, and real-time anomaly detection.

Fenwick (BIT)Segment TreeSparse TableSQRT Decomp
StructureBuildQueryUpdateRange updateMemory
O(n)O(log n)O(log n)O(log n) with 2 BITsn
O(n)O(log n)O(log n)O(log n) lazy4n
O(n log n)O(1)N/A (immutable)N/An log n
O(n)O(sqrt(n))O(sqrt(n))O(sqrt(n))n

sections.title: 7. Tries, Suffix Trees & String Algorithms
sections.description:
TRIES · SUFFIX ARRAYS · STRING SEARCH · AUTOCOMPLETE

Staff+ expectation: String algorithms power search, autocomplete, spell check, and bioinformatics at FAANG scale. At Staff+, you understand the full hierarchy: from simple tries (autocomplete, O(L) lookup) to suffix trees (all substring operations in O(m + |P|)) and suffix arrays (compressed, cache-friendly).

Trie (Prefix Tree): Store strings by their prefixes. O(L) lookup where L is string length. Compressed trie (radix tree) merges single-child nodes to save memory. Staff+ variants: (1) Bitwise trie for XOR queries (maximum XOR pair, O(32) per operation). (2) Suffix trie builds over all suffixes of a string — O(n^2) space, impractical. (3) Compressed suffix trie = suffix tree, O(n) space. At FAANG, tries power autocomplete (Google Search, 1B+ queries/day), IP routing (longest prefix match in O(32)), and spell checking.

Suffix Array + LCP Array: The workhorse for string problems at FAANG. Build in O(n log n) (Manber-Myers or prefix-doubling) or O(n) (SA-IS, DC3). LCP array in O(n) (Kasai's algorithm). String queries with SA + LCP + RMQ: (1) Pattern search — O(|P| + log n) with binary search on SA. (2) Longest repeating substring — max LCP. (3) Longest common substring of two strings — build SA over combined string, scan adjacent suffixes from different strings. (4) Distinct substrings — count = total substrings - sum(LCP).

TrieRadix treeSuffix arraySuffix treeAho-Corasick
StructureBuildSearchSpaceUse case
O(NL)O(L)O(NL)Autocomplete, dictionary
O(NL)O(L)O(N)IP routing, HTTP router
O(n log n)O(P + log n)O(n)Text indexing
O(n)O(P)O(n)Bioinformatics
O(NL)O(P + K)O(NL)Multi-pattern search

Aho-Corasick Automaton: Build a trie with failure links (similar to KMP's LPS). Search all patterns in a text in O(n + K) where K is number of matches. At FAANG: used for malware signature detection, content filtering, profanity filtering, and DPI (deep packet inspection). Staff+ insight: Aho-Corasick can be memory-intensive for large pattern sets. Optimize by using double-array trie (Darts-clone, used by Go's regex engine) or using BM25-style filtering before automaton search.

sections.title: 8. Hashing: Consistent, Rolling, Locality-Sensitive & Bloom Filters
sections.description:
HASHING · BLOOM FILTER · CONSISTENT HASH · LSH

Staff+ expectation: Hashing is the hidden backbone of every distributed system at FAANG. At Staff+, you need to understand hash function choice (speed vs collision resistance), probabilistic data structures, and how hashing enables distributed scaling. The wrong hash function can take down a service (hash-collision DoS attack).

Consistent Hashing: The fundamental technology behind data partitioning in distributed systems (DynamoDB, Cassandra, Redis Cluster, ScyllaDB). Each node is assigned one or more points on a hash ring. A key is stored on the first node whose hash is >= key's hash. When nodes are added or removed, only O(1/N) of keys need to move. Virtual nodes improve load distribution. Staff+ insight: the hash ring must use a balanced hash function (MD5, SHA-1). Use "rendezvous hashing" (highest random weight, HRW) as an alternative — no virtual nodes needed, and it supports weighted servers naturally.

Bloom Filters: Space-efficient probabilistic data structure. Trade off: false positives allowed, false negatives impossible. Uses k hash functions over a bit array of size m. Each insert: set bits at all k hash positions. Each lookup: if any bit is 0, the element is definitely not present. Optimal parameters: k = (m/n) * ln(2) for false positive rate p = (1 - e^(-kn/m))^k. At FAANG: (1) Google Bigtable uses bloom filters to avoid disk lookups for non-existent rows (2x speedup). (2) Apache Cassandra uses bloom filters for SSTable membership. (3) Medium uses bloom filters for recommending non-read articles. (4) Bitcoin uses bloom filters for SPV wallets.

Bloom filterCuckoo filterCount-min sketchHyperLogLogMinHash
StructureSpace per itemFalse positiveFalse negativeUse case
~2 bytes at 1% FPYes (tunable)NoMembership test
~1.5 bytes at 1% FPYes (rehash adds)NoLike bloom + deletion
~4 bytesYes (overcount)NoFrequency estimation
~1.5 KBYes (~2%)NoCardinality counting
~1 KBYesNoSimilarity estimation

Cuckoo Filter (2014): Better than bloom: supports deletion, has lower space, and has better locality (uses cuckoo hash table). When full, existing items are evicted and relocated (like cuckoo bird eggs). FAANG adoption: Netflix uses cuckoo filters for cache deduplication.

Count-Min Sketch: Estimate frequency of items with sub-linear space. Two-dimensional array of counters: width w = ceil(e / epsilon), depth d = ceil(ln(1 / delta)). Update: increment counter at (h_i(x), i) for each hash. Query: return minimum of counters. At FAANG: heavy hitter detection, network traffic monitoring, database query frequency estimation.

HyperLogLog:  Estimate distinct count with ~2% error using ~1.5 KB of memory regardless of cardinality. Based on observation: probability of seeing a hash with k leading zeros is 2^(-k). At FAANG: Google BigQuery uses HLL for approximate count distinct. Redis has native HLL support for analytics pipelines.

sections.title: 9. Greedy & Divide & Conquer at Scale
sections.description:
GREEDY · DIVIDE & CONQUER · OPTIMALITY · RECURRENCE

Staff+ expectation: Greedy algorithms are easy to get wrong and hard to prove correct. At Staff+, you can prove optimality using the exchange argument or greedy stays ahead. Divide & Conquer is the foundation of parallelism and distributed computing — map-reduce is D&C at internet scale.

Greedy Proof Techniques: (1) Exchange argument — starting from optimal solution, swap greedy choice and show no decrease in quality. Classic: interval scheduling, fractional knapsack, Huffman coding. (2) Greedy stays ahead — show the greedy choice is never worse than optimal at each step. Classic: task scheduling with deadlines (Earliest Deadline First, EDF). (3) Matroid theory — when a problem can be modeled as a matroid (finite set + independent subsets), greedy always gives optimal solution (Kruskal's MST, maximum weight independent set in a matroid).

Divide & Conquer at Scale: Merge sort, quicksort, and FFT are textbook. At FAANG scale: (1) External sorting — mergesort variant where data doesn't fit in memory. Phase 1: sort chunks and write to disk. Phase 2: merge sorted chunks with k-way merge (heap). (2) TeraSort — Hadoop's standard benchmark, uses partition sampling for balanced output. (3) Quickselect (Hoare's selection algorithm) — O(n) average to find k-th smallest. Used at Google for median-finding in streaming systems. (4) MapReduce as D&C — Mapper splits data (divide), Reducer merges results (conquer).

Huffman codingInterval schedulingKruskal's MSTQuickselectFFTClosest pair
AlgorithmTimeSpaceTechniqueFAANG use
O(n log n)O(n)Greedy + priority queueData compression
O(n log n)O(1)Earliest finish time firstTask scheduling
O(E log E)O(E)Greedy + DSUCluster merging
O(n) avgO(1)D&C + partitioningMedian finding
O(n log n)O(n)D&C + recursionSignal processing
O(n log n)O(n)D&C + strip mergeMap: nearest points

Master Theorem: For T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n): if f(n) = O(n^(log_b(a) - epsilon)), T(n) = Theta(n^(log_b(a))). If f(n) = Theta(n^(log_b(a)) * log^k n), T(n) = Theta(n^(log_b(a)) * log^(k+1) n). If f(n) = Omega(n^(log_b(a) + epsilon)), T(n) = Theta(f(n)). Staff+ insight: the master theorem works for 99% of textbook recurrences. For non-standard ones (T(n) = sqrt(n) T(sqrt(n)) + n), use substitution or recursion tree.

sections.title: 10. Concurrency & Parallel Algorithms
sections.description:
CONCURRENCY · PARALLEL · THREAD-SAFETY · LOCK-FREE

Staff+ expectation: Concurrency is the hardest topic in software engineering. At Staff+, you design thread-safe data structures, reason about memory models, and build parallel algorithms that scale linearly with cores. FAANG interviewers test concurrency through design problems: "design a thread-safe rate limiter," "implement a concurrent cache," "what happens when 1000 threads call this method simultaneously?"

Concurrency vs Parallelism: Concurrency is about dealing with many things at once (structure). Parallelism is about doing many things at once (execution). Concurrency enables parallelism, but they're not the same. At FAANG: Go is designed for concurrency (goroutines + channels). Java has the ForkJoinPool for parallelism. Python has asyncio (concurrency) and multiprocessing (parallelism, but at the cost of IPC overhead).

Parallel Algorithm Patterns: (1) Map — apply function to each element independently. (2) Reduce — combine results using associative operation (sum, max, AND). (3) Scan (prefix sum) — O(n/p + log p) with p processors using Hillis-Steele or work-efficient Blelloch scan. (4) Parallel sort — bitonic sort (O(log^2 n) parallel time), sample sort (partition then sort). (5) Parallel search — Monte Carlo tree search, parallel BFS (frontier-based). At FAANG, GPU-based parallelism uses CUDA or Vulkan compute shaders for ML training and inference.

Data raceDeadlockStarvationFalse sharingABA problem
ChallengeProblemMitigationFAANG tool
Multiple threads write same varMutex, atomic, RCUAtomicRef, RwLock
Circular lock waitLock ordering, try_lockWound-wait, wait-die
Thread never gets resourceFair locks, work-stealingForkJoinPool
Cache line ping-pongPadding, per-thread storageThreadLocal, alignas(64)
CAS on reused pointerTagged pointer, hazardRCU, epoch-based

Lock-Free Data Structures: The Staff+ standard for concurrent data structures. Tradeoffs: (1) Lock-based — easier to implement, risk of deadlock, priority inversion, convoying. (2) Lock-free — no deadlock, progress guaranteed (at least one thread makes progress). (3) Wait-free — every thread completes in bounded steps. Real-world: Java's ConcurrentLinkedQueue is lock-free (Michael-Scott). Go's map is not concurrent-safe by design. At FAANG, lock-free structures are used in hot paths where millions of ops/sec are needed.

Read-Copy-Update (RCU): The gold standard for read-mostly data structures. Readers proceed without locks (no atomic ops). Writers: copy, modify, wait for all readers to finish, then swap. Linux kernel uses RCU for the routing table, directory cache, and security contexts. At Meta, RCU is used in production garbage collectors and concurrent maps.

sections.title: 11. Memory Hierarchy-Aware Algorithms
sections.description:
MEMORY HIERARCHY · CACHE · LOCALITY · NUMA

Staff+ expectation: Memory is the new bottleneck. CPU speeds have increased 1000x since 2000, but memory latency has only improved 10x. At Staff+, you design algorithms that respect the memory hierarchy: L1 (<1ns, 32KB), L2 (<5ns, 256KB), L3 (<15ns, 8-20MB), RAM (<100ns), SSD (<100us), network (<10ms). An algorithm that is cache-oblivious can outperform a naive O(n log n) algorithm in practice.

Cache-Oblivious Algorithms: An algorithm is cache-oblivious if it performs well without knowing cache parameters (cache size, block size, associativity). The key technique: recursive divide-and-conquer that works on progressively smaller subproblems until each fits in cache. Example: (1) Cache-oblivious matrix multiplication using recursive blocking (Z-order / Morton order layout), O(n^3) time but O(n^3 / B * sqrt(M)) cache misses vs O(n^3 / B) for naive. (2) Cache-oblivious B-tree — search in O(log_B n) transfers, without knowing B. (3) Cache-oblivious sorting — funnelsort, optimal cache misses of O(n/B * log_(M/B) (n/B)).

NUMA Awareness (Non-Uniform Memory Access): At FAANG scale, servers have multiple sockets, each with local memory. Accessing remote memory (another socket) is 1.5-2x slower. NUMA-aware design: (1) Bind threads to cores (CPU pinning). (2) Allocate memory local to the thread's socket (mbind, set_mempolicy). (3) Use per-socket data structures (partitioned global address space). Google's Tensor Processing Unit (TPU) topology is NUMA-aware: 2D mesh interconnect with local HBM memory per chip.

L1 cacheL2 cacheL3 cacheRAM (DDR5)SSD (NVMe)Network
LevelSizeLatencyBWScoped to
32KB<1ns~1 TB/sPer core
256KB3-5ns~500 GB/sPer core
8-20MB10-15ns~200 GB/sShared socket
128-512GB80-100ns~50 GB/sSocket local
1-30TB10-100us~5 GB/sMachine local
Unlimited0.5-50ms1-100 GbpsCross-machine

False Sharing: When two threads modify variables on the same cache line, the cache coherence protocol invalidates the line between both threads every write. At 100M ops/sec, false sharing can cause 10x slowdown. Detection: perf c2c (Linux), Intel Vtune. Fix: pad data structures to cache line boundaries (64 bytes on x86, 128 bytes on Arm Apple M-series).

Staff+ Strategy: (1) Profile before optimizing — use perf, Valgrind, Cachegrind. (2) Prefer sequential access (cache-friendly). (3) Use structure-of-arrays (SoA) over array-of-structures (AoS) for SIMD. (4) For random access, use binary search over blocks (B-tree) not pointers (linked list). (5) When data doesn't fit in L3, the bottleneck moves from compute to memory bandwidth — this changes algorithm choice entirely.
sections.codeBlock:
# Cache-oblivious matrix multiplication (recursive blocking)
def matmul_cache_oblivious(A, B, size=None):
    """A, B are square matrices. Recursively splits until base case."""
    n = len(A)
    if size is None:
        size = n
    C = [[0] * n for _ in range(n)]

    def multiply(A, B, C, n, row_a, col_a, row_b, col_b, row_c, col_c):
        if n <= 64:  # base case: fits in L1
            for i in range(n):
                for k in range(n):
                    aik = A[row_a + i][col_a + k]
                    for j in range(n):
                        C[row_c + i][col_c + j] += aik * B[row_b + k][col_b + j]
            return

        mid = n // 2
        # Recursively multiply 8 sub-problems
        multiply(A, B, C, mid, row_a, col_a, row_b, col_b, row_c, col_c)
        multiply(A, B, C, mid, row_a, col_a, row_b, col_b + mid, row_c, col_c + mid)
        multiply(A, B, C, mid, row_a + mid, col_a, row_b, col_b, row_c + mid, col_c)
        multiply(A, B, C, mid, row_a + mid, col_a, row_b, col_b + mid, row_c + mid, col_c + mid)
        multiply(A, B, C, mid, row_a, col_a + mid, row_b + mid, col_b, row_c, col_c)
        multiply(A, B, C, mid, row_a, col_a + mid, row_b + mid, col_b + mid, row_c, col_c + mid)
        multiply(A, B, C, mid, row_a + mid, col_a + mid, row_b + mid, col_b, row_c + mid, col_c)
        multiply(A, B, C, mid, row_a + mid, col_a + mid, row_b + mid, col_b + mid, row_c + mid, col_c + mid)

    multiply(A, B, C, n, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0)
    return C
    # Cache misses: O(n^3 / B * sqrt(M)) vs O(n^3 / B) for naive

# Array-of-Structures vs Structure-of-Arrays
# AoS (bad for SIMD/cache)
class PointAoS:
    def __init__(self, x, y, z):
        self.x = x; self.y = y; self.z = z
# Access: points[i].x, points[i].y, points[i].z
# Only 1/3 of cache line used when iterating x only

# SoA (good for SIMD/cache)
class PointsSoA:
    def __init__(self, n):
        self.x = [0] * n
        self.y = [0] * n
        self.z = [0] * n
# Access: xs[i], ys[i], zs[i]
# Full cache lines used when iterating x only

# Cache line padding to avoid false sharing
import threading

class PaddedCounter:
    def __init__(self):
        self.value = 0
        self._pad1 = 0
        self._pad2 = 0
        self._pad3 = 0
        self._pad4 = 0
        self._pad5 = 0
        self._pad6 = 0
        self._pad7 = 0  # ~64 bytes total

# Simulating impact of memory locality
import time

def sum_fast(arr):
    """Sequential access: uses prefetching"""
    total = 0
    for x in arr:
        total += x
    return total

def sum_slow(arr):
    """Random access: cache miss on every access"""
    total = 0
    i = 0
    n = len(arr)
    for _ in range(n):
        total += arr[i]
        i = (i + 123456) % n  # pseudorandom stride
    return total

# On a modern CPU with N=10M ints:
# sum_fast: ~15ms (sequential, prefetcher works)
# sum_slow: ~120ms (8x slower, cache misses)

sections.title: 12. Randomized Algorithms: Monte Carlo, Sampling & Streaming
sections.description:
RANDOMIZED · MONTE CARLO · SAMPLING · STREAMING

Staff+ expectation: Randomized algorithms are essential at FAANG scale. Deterministic exact solutions are impossible or impractical for massive datasets. At Staff+, you know when to use randomization to trade accuracy for speed, and how to bound error probabilities.

Monte Carlo vs Las Vegas: Monte Carlo — randomized algorithm with deterministic runtime but bounded error probability (e.g., Karger's min-cut, Freivalds' matrix multiplication check). Las Vegas — always correct but runtime is random (e.g., randomized quicksort, random walk on graphs). Staff+ insight: in production, Las Vegas is preferred when possible (correctness is guaranteed). Monte Carlo is used when exact solutions are infeasible (e.g., Monte Carlo Tree Search in AlphaGo, probabilistic model checking).

Reservoir Sampling: Select k items uniformly at random from a stream of unknown length n. Algorithm: keep first k items. For item i > k, replace a random element with probability k/i. Each element has probability k/n of being in the final sample. Staff+ variant: weighted reservoir sampling (each item has a weight), distributed reservoir sampling (merge samples from multiple partitions), and reservoir sampling with deleted items (maintain reservoir + count of deletions).

Streaming Algorithms: Process data in one pass with O(log n) or O(1) memory. (1) Count-Min Sketch — frequency estimation (covered in section 8). (2) HyperLogLog — distinct count. (3) T-Digest — streaming percentiles. (4) Misra-Gries — frequent items (heavy hitters, find all elements appearing > 1/k of stream). At FAANG: streaming algorithms power real-time dashboards (Google Analytics, Cloud Monitoring), anomaly detection (Netflix Chaos Monkey), and A/B test analysis.

Reservoir samplingMisra-GriesT-DigestAMS sketchHyperLogLog
AlgorithmProblemSpaceErrorUse case
Uniform sample from streamO(k)None (exact)A/B test sampling
Frequent items (heavy hitters)O(k)UnderestimateTop-k trending topics
Streaming percentilesO(k)~1%Latency p99 monitoring
Moment estimationO(log n)~10%Database statistics
Distinct count~1.5KB~2%Unique visitor count

Randomized Approximation: (1) Freivalds' algorithm — verify matrix multiplication A*B = C by checking A*(Br) = Cr for random vector r. O(n^2) vs O(n^3) deterministic. Error: 1/2 per trial. Repeat k times: error ≤ 2^(-k). (2) Karger's min-cut — randomly contract edges; probability of finding min-cut is at least 2/(n^2). Repeat O(n^2 log n) times for high probability. Used at Google for network reliability analysis. (3) Randomized rounding — solve LP relaxation, randomly round fractional solutions to integral ones. Used in approximation algorithms for NP-hard problems (set cover, MAX-CUT, scheduling).

sections.title: 13. Online Algorithms & Competitive Analysis
sections.description:
ONLINE ALGORITHMS · COMPETITIVE RATIO · ADVERSARIAL

Staff+ expectation: Online algorithms make decisions without knowing the future. At FAANG, nearly every system is online: load balancers route requests without knowing which connections arrive next, caches evict without knowing future accesses, ad servers bid without knowing future bids. Staff+ engineers design online algorithms and reason about their competitive ratio (how much worse than optimal offline?).

Competitive Analysis: An online algorithm is c-competitive if cost(on ALG) ≤ c * cost(on OPT) + alpha for all inputs. c is the competitive ratio. Example: (1) List update (move-to-front) is 2-competitive. (2) Ski rental is e/(e-1)-competitive for randomized, 2-competitive for deterministic. (3) Paging (cache eviction) — LRU is k-competitive (k = cache size). At Staff+, you prove competitive ratios to justify algorithm choices.

Key Online Algorithms at FAANG: (1) LRU vs LFU — LRU is k-competitive for paging. LFU is not competitive (adversary can request a single rarely-used page repeatedly). Real-world: Redis uses an approximation of LFU for key eviction (because it has better average-case behavior). (2) Random — surprisingly, random performs well online. Random paging is O(log k)-competitive (better than deterministic LRU's k-competitiveness). (3) Multiplicative Weights Update (MWU) — the "hedge" algorithm for online learning. Used at Google for ad placement, A/B test allocation, and adaptive routing.

Paging/cachingPaging (random)Ski rental (cloud)List updateMTS (k-server)Ad serving
ProblemOnline algorithmCompetitive ratioFAANG use
LRUkMemcache, Redis, CDN
RAND markerO(log k)Cloudflare, Fastly
Randomizede/(e-1)Spot instance bidding
Move-to-front2Auto-complete reorder
Work func (WFA)2k-1CDN server placement
MWU (hedge)Equals best expertGoogle Ads

Online Convex Optimization (OCO): Framework for online decision-making where loss functions are convex. (1) Online Gradient Descent — O(sqrt(T)) regret in convex setting. (2) Follow the Regularized Leader (FTRL) — used by Google for online ad CTR prediction (FTRL-Proximal, McMahan et al. 2013). (3) Exp3 — for non-convex, adversarial settings with multi-armed bandits. At FAANG: OCO drives real-time bidding, news feed ranking, recommendation diversification, and adaptive rate limiting.

Adversarial vs Stochastic: Online algorithms are evaluated in two models: adversarial (worst-case input, assumed in interviews) and stochastic (i.i.d. input, closer to real-world). Staff+ insight: real-world traffic is neither fully adversarial nor purely stochastic. Use smoothed analysis (small random perturbations of adversarial input) for more realistic bounds. At FAANG, A/B test traffic is near-stochastic, but DoS attacks are adversarial — design for both.
sections.codeBlock:
# LRU cache (online paging algorithm) — O(1) operations
class LRUCache:
    def __init__(self, capacity):
        self.capacity = capacity
        self.cache = {}  # key -> node
        # Doubly linked list: most recent <-> least recent
        self.head = Node(0, 0)  # dummy
        self.tail = Node(0, 0)  # dummy
        self.head.next = self.tail
        self.tail.prev = self.head

    def get(self, key):
        if key in self.cache:
            node = self.cache[key]
            self._move_to_front(node)
            return node.value
        return -1

    def put(self, key, value):
        if key in self.cache:
            node = self.cache[key]
            node.value = value
            self._move_to_front(node)
        else:
            if len(self.cache) >= self.capacity:
                lru = self.tail.prev
                self._remove(lru)
                del self.cache[lru.key]
            node = Node(key, value)
            self.cache[key] = node
            self._add_to_front(node)

    def _add_to_front(self, node):
        node.prev = self.head
        node.next = self.head.next
        self.head.next.prev = node
        self.head.next = node

    def _remove(self, node):
        node.prev.next = node.next
        node.next.prev = node.prev

    def _move_to_front(self, node):
        self._remove(node)
        self._add_to_front(node)
    # LRU is k-competitive for paging (k = cache size)

class Node:
    def __init__(self, key, value):
        self.key = key
        self.value = value
        self.prev = None
        self.next = None

# Online gradient descent (OCO)
import random
import math

def online_gradient_descent(queries, T, learning_rate_fn):
    """queries: iterable of (feature, true_label) pairs
    Returns: regret (cumulative loss difference from best static model)
    """
    theta = [0.0]  # model parameter (scalar for simplicity)
    total_loss = 0.0
    best_loss = float('inf')

    for t in range(T):
        x, y = queries[t] if isinstance(queries[t], tuple) else (1.0, queries[t])
        # Predict
        pred = theta[0] * x
        loss = (pred - y) ** 2  # squared loss
        total_loss += loss

        # Gradient of squared loss: 2 * (pred - y) * x
        gradient = 2 * (pred - y) * x

        # Update
        eta = learning_rate_fn(t)
        theta[0] -= eta * gradient

        # Track best static model in hindsight
        # (simplified: just compare to zero predictor)
        static_loss = y ** 2
        best_loss = min(best_loss, static_loss)

    regret = total_loss - best_loss
    # OCO guarantees regret = O(sqrt(T)) for convex losses
    return regret

# Multiplicative Weights Update (hedge)
def multiplicative_weights(n_experts, T, losses, eta=0.1):
    """n_experts: number of strategies
    T: time horizon
    losses: [T][n_experts] array of losses in [0,1]
    Returns: cumulative loss of MWU vs best expert
    """
    weights = [1.0] * n_experts
    total_loss = 0.0
    best_expert_loss = [0.0] * n_experts

    for t in range(T):
        # Normalize weights
        total_w = sum(weights)
        probs = [w / total_w for w in weights]

        # Randomly choose expert according to distribution
        chosen = random.choices(range(n_experts), weights=probs)[0]

        # Suffer loss
        loss = losses[t][chosen]
        total_loss += loss

        # Update weights (exponential weighting)
        for i in range(n_experts):
            weights[i] *= math.exp(-eta * losses[t][i])
            best_expert_loss[i] += losses[t][i]

    regret = total_loss - min(best_expert_loss)
    # MWU guarantees regret <= O(sqrt(T log n))
    return regret, total_loss

sections.title: 14. NP-Completeness & Approximation Algorithms
sections.description:
NP-COMPLETENESS · APPROXIMATION · REDUCTIONS

Staff+ expectation: Not all problems have efficient solutions. At Staff+, you recognize NP-hard problems, prove NP-completeness when needed, and design approximation algorithms for real-world instances. At FAANG, many production systems solve NP-hard problems approximately: scheduling (warehouse robots at Amazon), routing (Google Maps), recommendation (Netflix prize), chip design (EDA at Apple). Staff+ insight: practical instances of NP-hard problems are rarely worst-case. Use heuristics + verify with bounds.

Proving NP-Completeness (Reduction Chain): To show problem X is NP-complete: (1) Show X is in NP (can verify a solution in polynomial time). (2) Reduce a known NP-complete problem Y to X in polynomial time. Classic reductions: SAT → 3SAT → Independent Set → Vertex Cover → Clique → Hamiltonian Path → TSP. Cook-Levin theorem: SAT is NP-complete. Staff+ insight: in interviews, you rarely need to prove NP-completeness from scratch. Recognize the pattern: "this looks like knapsack/partition/set cover" → "it's NP-hard" → "here's an approximation or special-case solution."

Approximation Algorithms: (1) Vertex Cover — 2-approximation: take both endpoints of every uncovered edge. Simple, optimal for many instances (at FAANG: network monitoring point placement). (2) Set Cover — O(log n)-approximation: greedy pick set covering most uncovered elements. At Google: sensor placement, test case selection. (3) MAX-CUT — 0.5-approximation (randomized): assign each vertex randomly to left or right. Expected cut size ≥ 0.5 * optimal. Goemans-Williamson gives 0.878-approximation using semidefinite programming. (4) TSP: Christofides algorithm gives 1.5-approximation for metric TSP. Google Maps uses CONCORDE solver for exact TSP on up to 1000 nodes.

Vertex coverSet coverMAX-CUTTSP (metric)KnapsackSteiner tree
ProblemApproximationTechniquePractical use
2Greedy maximal matchingNetwork monitoring
O(log n)Greedy (max uncovered)Sensor placement
0.878 (GW)SDP roundingVLSI design
1.5 (Christofides)MST + matchingLogistics routing
(1+epsilon)DP + rounding (FPTAS)Budget allocation
1.39 (by)LP + iterative roundingCable routing

FPTAS (Fully Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme): For some NP-hard problems (knapsack, subset-sum), there is an algorithm that gives a (1+epsilon)-approximation in time polynomial in n and 1/epsilon. Staff+ insight: in practice, FPTAS makes NP-hard problems tractable for reasonable epsilon (0.01-0.1). At FAANG: knapsack FPTAS is used for ad budget allocation (choose ads within budget to maximize CTR), portfolio optimization, and resource allocation.

Parameterized Complexity: Some problems are NP-hard but fixed-parameter tractable (FPT): O(f(k) * n^O(1)) where k is a small parameter (solution size, treewidth, maximum degree). Example: (1) Vertex cover — O(1.618^k + n) using kernelization + branching. (2) k-path (find path of length k) — O(2^k * n^O(1)) using color-coding. At FAANG: parameterized algorithms are used when the "hard part" of the problem is small — e.g., finding the k most influential nodes in a social network, where k is small (10-100) but n is billions.
sections.codeBlock:
# 2-approximation for vertex cover
import random

def vertex_cover_approx(graph):
    """graph: adjacency list {v: [neighbors]}
    Returns a vertex cover of size at most 2 * optimal
    """
    edges = set()
    for u in graph:
        for v in graph[u]:
            if u < v:
                edges.add((u, v))

    cover = set()
    uncovered = set(edges)
    while uncovered:
        u, v = uncovered.pop()
        cover.add(u)
        cover.add(v)
        # Remove all edges incident to u or v
        to_remove = set()
        for e in uncovered:
            if e[0] in (u, v) or e[1] in (u, v):
                to_remove.add(e)
        uncovered -= to_remove
    return cover
    # Proof: optimal vertex cover must include at least
    # one endpoint of each edge. We take both -> 2-approximation

# Greedy set cover (O(log n)-approximation)
def set_cover(universe, subsets):
    """universe: set of all elements
    subsets: list of sets
    Returns list of subset indices covering all elements
    """
    uncovered = set(universe)
    chosen = []
    subset_list = list(subsets)

    while uncovered:
        best_idx = max(range(len(subset_list)),
                       key=lambda i: len(uncovered & subset_list[i]))
        chosen.append(best_idx)
        uncovered -= subset_list[best_idx]
        subset_list[best_idx] = set()  # prevent re-selection

    return chosen
    # O(log n) approximation ratio is tight (matches inapproximability)

# FPTAS for 0/1 knapsack
def knapsack_fptas(weights, values, capacity, epsilon):
    """(1+epsilon)-approximation for 0/1 knapsack
    Time: O(n^2 / epsilon), Space: O(n^2 / epsilon)
    """
    n = len(weights)
    max_val = max(values)
    # Scale values
    scaling = epsilon * max_val / n
    scaled_values = [int(v / scaling) for v in values]

    max_scaled = sum(scaled_values)
    dp = [float('inf')] * (max_scaled + 1)
    dp[0] = 0

    for i in range(n):
        for v in range(max_scaled, scaled_values[i] - 1, -1):
            dp[v] = min(dp[v], dp[v - scaled_values[i]] + weights[i])

    best_val = 0
    for v in range(max_scaled + 1):
        if dp[v] <= capacity:
            best_val = v

    return int(best_val * scaling)
    # Guarantee: (1+epsilon)-approximation

# Goemans-Williamson for MAX-CUT (conceptual 0.878-approx)
# Real implementation uses SDP solver

def max_cut_random(graph):
    """Simple randomized 0.5-approximation"""
    n = len(graph)
    assignment = [random.choice([-1, 1]) for _ in range(n)]
    cut_size = 0
    for u in range(n):
        for v in graph[u]:
            if u < v and assignment[u] != assignment[v]:
                cut_size += 1
    return cut_size, assignment
    # E[cut] >= |E|/2 >= OPT/2 (since OPT <= |E|)

sections.title: 15. DSA in System Design Context
sections.description:
DSA × SYSTEM DESIGN · DISTRIBUTED · REAL-WORLD

Staff+ expectation: Data structures and algorithms don't exist in isolation. At Staff+, you recognize how fundamental DSA maps to real-world system design decisions. The same algorithm that solves an interview problem is the foundation of a multi-billion-dollar system.

Consistent Hashing (DynamoDB, Cassandra, Redis Cluster): Distributed hash table using hash ring + virtual nodes. Interview version: "design a load balancer that minimizes rebalancing." Production: Amazon DynamoDB (2012) uses consistent hashing for partition distribution. When a node fails, only 1/N of keys move (vs N-1 for naive modulo). The interview implementation is a simplified version of Dynamo's partitioning logic.

LSM Tree (LevelDB, Bigtable, Cassandra, RocksDB): Write-optimized data structure. Memtable (in-memory sorted structure) + immutable SSTables on disk. Compaction merges SSTables to maintain read performance. Interview: "design a key-value store with high write throughput." Production: Bigtable (Google, 2006) popularized LSM trees. LevelDB/RocksDB (Meta) power millions of databases. The B-tree vs LSM tree tradeoff (covered in section 3) is a Staff+ design decision.

Bloom Filters (Bigtable, Cassandra, Bitcoin): Probabilistic set membership. Interview: "design a system to avoid disk lookups for non-existent keys." Production: Bigtable uses bloom filters per SSTable to skip disk I/O for non-existent rows. In Bitcoin, bloom filters enable SPV wallets to verify transactions without downloading the full blockchain. Staff+ insight: when designing caches, bloom filters reduce cache stampede (ask "is this key definitely not in the DB?" before checking DB).

Design a KV storeDesign a rate limiterDesign a search engineDesign a news feedDesign a priority queueDesign a unique ID gen
Interview ProblemData StructureProduction SystemFAANG
LSM tree, merge iteratorLevelDB, RocksDBGoogle, Meta
Token bucket, sliding windowRedis / Guava RateLimiterAll FAANG
Inverted index, TF-IDF, PageRankGoogle SearchGoogle
Graph, fan-out, rankingFacebook feed rankingMeta
Binary heap, skip listKafka partitionsLinkedIn, Confluent
Snowflake, TSP, rangeSnowflake, UIDGeneratorTwitter, Instagram

Merge Iterators (LSM trees, Bigtable, LevelDB): Merge multiple sorted iterators into one sorted output. Binary heap of iterators (k-way merge). Each pop: extract min from heap, advance that iterator, push next value. Interview: "merge k sorted lists." Production: merging SSTables in LSM compaction, merging inverted index segments in search engines, merging event streams in Kafka Streams.

Binary Search in Databases: B-tree internal nodes guide binary search to the correct leaf. B+ trees improve on B-trees by only storing data in leaves, enabling more internal nodes per page (higher fanout, fewer I/Os). Interview: "search in a sorted array." Production: B+ tree search in PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, and MongoDB WiredTiger. The "binary search on a sorted array" is a B+ tree of height 1 (root node point to data leaf).

Graph Traversal in Distributed Systems: BFS → gossip protocol (Cassandra, Dynamo). Dijkstra → OSPF routing. Topological sort → build systems (Bazel, Buck, Pants). Strongly connected components → circular dependency detection in package managers. Interview: "detect cycle in a graph." Production: npm's dependency resolution (circular dependencies are common in npm — developers often don't realize).
sections.codeBlock:
# k-way merge (merge k sorted lists)
import heapq

def merge_k_sorted(lists):
    """k sorted iterators -> one sorted iterator
    Used in: LSM tree compaction, inverted index merge, stream join
    """
    heap = []
    for i, lst in enumerate(lists):
        if lst:
            heapq.heappush(heap, (lst[0], i, 0))

    result = []
    while heap:
        val, list_idx, elem_idx = heapq.heappop(heap)
        result.append(val)
        if elem_idx + 1 < len(lists[list_idx]):
            heapq.heappush(heap, (lists[list_idx][elem_idx + 1], list_idx, elem_idx + 1))
    return result
    # Time: O(N log k), Space: O(k)

# Token bucket rate limiter (foundation of all FAANG rate limiters)
import time
import threading

class TokenBucket:
    def __init__(self, rate, burst):
        """rate: tokens per second, burst: max tokens"""
        self.rate = rate
        self.burst = burst
        self.tokens = burst
        self.last_refill = time.monotonic()
        self.lock = threading.Lock()

    def allow_request(self, tokens=1):
        with self.lock:
            now = time.monotonic()
            elapsed = now - self.last_refill
            self.tokens = min(self.burst, self.tokens + elapsed * self.rate)
            self.last_refill = now

            if self.tokens >= tokens:
                self.tokens -= tokens
                return True
            return False
    # Used by: Google Cloud API Gateway, AWS API Gateway, Kong

# Consistent hashing (load balancing / partitioning)
# (Full implementation in Section 8)
# Key insight: virtual nodes + hash ring = DynamoDB partitioning

# Topological sort for build systems
# (Full implementation in Section 4)
# Key insight: Bazel uses Kahn's algorithm for dependency resolution

# Inverted index (search engine foundation)
class InvertedIndex:
    def __init__(self):
        self.index = {}  # term -> [(doc_id, frequency), ...]
        self.doc_count = 0

    def add_document(self, text):
        doc_id = self.doc_count
        self.doc_count += 1
        terms = text.lower().split()
        term_freq = {}
        for term in terms:
            term_freq[term] = term_freq.get(term, 0) + 1
        for term, freq in term_freq.items():
            if term not in self.index:
                self.index[term] = []
            self.index[term].append((doc_id, freq))

    def search(self, query):
        terms = query.lower().split()
        if not terms:
            return []
        # Intersect posting lists
        result = set(doc_id for doc_id, _ in self.index.get(terms[0], []))
        for term in terms[1:]:
            posting = set(doc_id for doc_id, _ in self.index.get(term, []))
            result &= posting
        return sorted(result)
    # Time: O(union of posting list lengths)

sections.title: 16. Staff+ DSA Interview Strategy
sections.description:
INTERVIEW STRATEGY · PATTERNS · MOCK

Staff+ expectation: At Staff+ level, DSA interviews test more than coding. Interviewers evaluate: (1) Can you communicate your thought process clearly? (2) Do you consider edge cases and tradeoffs? (3) Can you connect DSA to production systems? (4) Do you know when to approximate? FAANG Staff+ DSA rounds are 45-60 minutes, expect 1-2 problems (first is warm-up, second is the signal-builder).

Company-Specific Patterns: (1) Google: 1-2 DSA rounds (phone screen + on-site). Focus on arrays, strings, DP, graphs. Expect to code fully (shared doc / CoderPad). Google L5+ expects clean code with O(1) space when possible. (2) Meta: 1 DSA round (45 min). Strong focus on arrays, strings, recursion. Must finish 2 problems. Code must compile (Meta uses their own IDE). (3) Amazon: 1-2 DSA rounds. Focus on arrays, strings, and OOP design. Amazon L6+ expects you to talk through production tradeoffs (not just solve). (4) Stripe: 1 DSA round. Focus on API design and state machines. Stripe is unique: DSA is combined with API design. (5) Netflix: No dedicated DSA round (focus on system design and behavioral). But coding is expected in the work sample.

Phone screenOn-site round 1On-site round 2Staff+ (L6/E6)
StageTypical problemFocusTimeCoding
Medium (LC Medium)Problem solving + communication45 minShared doc
Easy to MediumWarm-up, coding speed45 minCoderPad
Medium to HardDepth, edge cases, optimization45-60 minFull IDE
Medium + system tie-inProduction tradeoffs, scaling60 minWhiteboard style

Pattern Recognition (14 Patterns): (1) Sliding window — subarray/substring with constraints. (2) Two pointers or iteration — sorted arrays, partitioning. (3) Fast and slow pointers — cycle detection, middle of linked list. (4) Merge intervals — interval intersection, meeting rooms. (5) Cyclic sort — array of 1..n with duplicates/missing. (6) In-place linked list reversal — palindrome, reverse nodes in k-group. (7) BFS — shortest path in unweighted graphs, tree level order. (8) DFS — all paths, tree depth, graph connectivity. (9) Two heaps — median of a stream, sliding window median. (10) Subsets — backtracking, combinations, permutations. (11) Modified binary search — rotated array, closest element. (12) Top K elements — heap, quickselect. (13) K-way merge — merge k sorted lists. (14) DP patterns — 0/1 knapsack, unbounded knapsack, LCS, LIS, palindrome partitioning.

Staff+ Interview Mindset: The interviewer is not looking for "can you solve this" but "would I want to work with you on a hard problem?" Show: (1) Clarify the problem repeat it in your own words. (2) Ask about constraints (input size, memory, data types, edge cases). (3) Propose brute force first, then optimize. (4) Write clean code with meaningful variable names. (5) Test with example, edge case (empty, single, duplicates), and performance test. (6) Discuss production implications: "this algorithm takes O(n log n) time, but if the data doesn't fit in memory, we'd use external sorting with O(n/B log n/B) I/Os."

Practice Plan for Staff+: (1) Solve 3 LC problems daily (mix of Easy/Medium/Hard). (2) Focus on Medium (80%) + Hard (20%). At Staff+, you must solve any Medium within 20 minutes. (3) Use LeetCode Discuss to see top-voted solutions — they reflect FAANG expectations. (4) Every problem: write brute force -> analyze bottlenecks -> optimize -> test. (5) 2x speed playback: watch yourself solving — is your communication clear? Do you get stuck on the same pattern? (6) Mock interviews: 5-10 with peers before the real thing.
sections.codeBlock:
# Problem-solving template for Staff+ interviews
from typing import List, Optional

class InterviewSolution:
    """Template used implicitly in FAANG Staff+ DSA rounds"""

    def solve(self, input_data):
        """
        1. CLARIFY: "Let me restate the problem to make sure I understand..."
        2. EDGE CASES: "What should I return for empty input? Single element?"
        3. BRUTE FORCE: "The naive approach is O(n^2) by checking every pair..."
        4. OPTIMIZE: "We can improve to O(n log n) by sorting and using two pointers..."
        5. CODE: Write clean, production-quality code
        6. TEST: "Let me trace through [example], edge case [empty], and [duplicates]"
        7. COMPLEXITY: "Time: O(n log n) due to sorting. Space: O(1) in-place."
        8. PRODUCTION: "In production, if data doesn't fit in memory, we'd use..."
        """
        pass

# Example: solve "find k closest elements" with Staff+ depth
def find_k_closest(arr: List[int], k: int, x: int) -> List[int]:
    """
    Clarify: sorted array, return k closest to x (ties -> smaller first)
    Constraints: len(arr) >= k, arr sorted ascending
    """
    # Binary search to find insertion point of x
    l, r = 0, len(arr) - k
    while l < r:
        mid = (l + r) // 2
        # Compare distance at mid vs mid + k
        if x - arr[mid] > arr[mid + k] - x:
            l = mid + 1
        else:
            r = mid
    return arr[l:l + k]
    # Time: O(log(n-k) + k), Space: O(1)
    # Production note: if arr doesn't fit in memory, use external binary search

# Time management: 45 min problem
# - Clarify + brute force: 5 min
# - Optimize + design: 10 min
# - Code: 20 min
# - Test + complexity + production: 10 min

# Common pitfalls at Staff+:
# 1. Not asking about constraints (especially input size)
# 2. Jumping to optimal solution without discussing tradeoffs
# 3. Writing buggy code (off-by-one, edge cases)
# 4. Not testing (always trace through at least one example)
# 5. Solving too fast (signals you memorized the problem)

# Mock interview practice structure
class MockSession:
    def __init__(self, role: str, year: int):
        self.role = role  # "L6 SWE", "E6", "Senior Staff"
        self.year = year
        self.problems_solved = 0
        self.patterns_missed = []

    def log_result(self, pattern: str, solved: bool, time_min: int):
        if not solved:
            self.patterns_missed.append(pattern)
        self.problems_solved += int(solved)

    def get_study_plan(self):
        weak_patterns = set(self.patterns_missed)
        return [f"Focus on: {p}" for p in weak_patterns]
    # Staff+ tip: weak patterns are your biggest leverage

sections.title: 17. References & Further Reading
sections.description:
REFERENCES · PAPERS · BOOKS

Staff+ expectation: Deep knowledge of DSA comes from reading the classics. Below are the papers, books, and resources that FAANG Staff+ engineers reference when designing data structures and algorithms for production systems.

Foundational Books:

  • CLRS — "Introduction to Algorithms" (4th ed): The definitive reference. Know chapters 1-25 (analysis, sorting, trees, graphs, DP, greedy, amortized analysis).
  • Steven Skiena — "The Algorithm Design Manual": Better for practical algorithm design. Includes a catalog of algorithmic problems.
  • Jeff Erickson — "Algorithms": Free online. Excellent for theoretical depth.
  • Robert Sedgewick & Kevin Wayne — "Algorithms": Strong on practical implementations and Java code.
  • Don Knuth — "The Art of Computer Programming" (vol 1-4A): The bible. Read selectively (sorting, searching, combinatorial algorithms).
  • Jon Bentley — "Programming Pearls": Classic on practical algorithm design for real-world constraints.

Essential Papers (every Staff+ should know):

  • Michael & Scott (1996) — "Simple, Fast, and Practical Non-Blocking and Blocking Concurrent Queue Algorithms": Lock-free queue foundation.
  • Blelloch (1990) — "Prefix Sums and Their Applications": Parallel prefix scan.
  • Manber & Myers (1993) — "Suffix Arrays: A New Method for On-Line String Searches": O(n log n) suffix array construction.
  • Karger & Stein (1996) — "A New Approach to the Minimum Cut Problem": Randomized min-cut.
  • Flajolet, Fusy, Gandouet & Meunier (2007) — "HyperLogLog: the analysis of a near-optimal cardinality estimation algorithm": Streaming cardinality.
  • Bonomi, Mitzenmacher et al. (2006) — "An Improved Construction for Counting Bloom Filters": Bloom filter variants.
  • Fredman & Tarjan (1987) — "Fibonacci Heaps and Their Uses in Improved Network Optimization Algorithms": O(1) decrease-key.
  • Dijkstra (1959) — "A Note on Two Problems in Connexion with Graphs": Shortest path algorithm.
  • Floyd (1962) — "Algorithm 97: Shortest Path": All-pairs shortest path.
  • Hoare (1961) — "Algorithm 63: Partition" and "Algorithm 64: Quicksort": Quicksort + quickselect.

Staff+ DSA Cheat Sheet (Quick Reference):

  • Sorting: O(n log n) comparison-based. O(n + k) counting. O(n log n/k) shell sort hybrid.
  • Search: Sorted array = O(log n) binary. Hash = O(1) avg. BST = O(log n) balanced, O(n) degenerate.
  • Graphs: Adjacency matrix (V^2) vs list (V+E). Choose based on density: E > V^2/log V = matrix.
  • DP: 4 steps — state, recurrence, base, order. Optimize: state compression, Divide & Conquer DP, Knuth, CHT.
  • Greedy: Exchange argument or greedy stays ahead. Matroids guarantee optimality.
  • Strings: Trie (autocomplete) vs Suffix array+RMQ (substring search) vs Aho-Corasick (multi-pattern).
  • Streaming: HLL (cardinality), CM sketch (frequency), TDigest (percentiles), Bloom (membership).
  • Probabilistic: Monte Carlo (bounded error) vs Las Vegas (bounded runtime).

Online Resources:

  • CP-Algorithms (cp-algorithms.com): Comprehensive algorithms reference with competitive programming depth.
  • VisuAlgo (visualgo.net): Visualize data structures and algorithms.
  • USACO Guide (usaco.guide): Competitive programming curriculum with excellent theory.
  • E-maxx (e-maxx.ru/algo): Russian algorithms archive (translated).
  • LeetCode Discuss: FAANG-specific solution patterns and interview experiences.
